quotes.quote: What this ends up leading to is a configuration management system for manufacturing, much like a build system.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle explores what it would take to build robot workshop assistants like those in Iron Man, breaking down the technical requirements for fire detection, object passing, and suit assembly. She then extends this into a broader concept: a configuration management system for manufacturing that treats physical factory layouts like code deployments. The key insight is that robotics workflows could be defined in declarative config files, enabling version-controlled 'modes' for different manufacturing processes.
quotes.key_insight: Manufacturing workflows could be treated like software deployments, with declarative configuration files defining robot positions, behaviors, and artifact pipelines that can be version-controlled and switched on command.

quotes.quote: What a fantastic world we live in that we can witness the first Apple-like keynote for batteries.
quotes.summary: This post provides a comprehensive technical deep-dive into battery technology, prompted by Tesla's 2020 Battery Day announcement. Frazelle explains the fundamentals of how batteries work—from the chemistry of redox reactions to the differences between alkaline and lithium-ion cells—before exploring emerging technologies like solid-state batteries, nuclear batteries, and silicon anodes. The bulk of the post analyzes Tesla's holistic approach to battery improvement, which addresses cell design, manufacturing processes, materials selection, and vehicle integration to achieve their goal of halving cost per kilowatt-hour.
quotes.key_insight: Tesla's battery improvements succeed because they take a holistic, first-principles approach—optimizing cell design, manufacturing, materials, and vehicle integration together rather than focusing on a single breakthrough technology.

quotes.quote: I joke so much that I automated our CIO that I even named the repo holding the code…cio.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle describes building an automated internal infrastructure system at Oxide Computer Company that consolidates data from various external services (Google Forms, GitHub, etc.) into a single database with an API layer on top. The system evolved from 'bash, popsicle sticks, glue, and some rust' into a cleaner Rust-based architecture that provides resilience against API rate limits and service downtime. The infrastructure handles everything from applicant tracking to RFD (Request for Discussion) document management, with Airtable serving as a visual frontend for the data.
quotes.key_insight: Owning your company's operational data in a centralized database with an API layer provides resilience, searchability, and the flexibility to build custom tooling on top of external services.

quotes.quote: I like to do a lot of research and I tend to want the best thing.
quotes.summary: This post provides a comprehensive technical overview of all seven ASTM-classified additive manufacturing processes, from FDM to sheet lamination, before comparing two consumer 3D printers: the MakerBot Replicator+ (FDM) and Form 3 (SLA). Frazelle's hands-on testing reveals the Form 3 produces significantly higher quality prints in less time, though the MakerBot offers better user experience with its iPad app and camera integration. The article reflects her characteristic approach of doing deep research before purchasing, wanting 'the best' thing that 'just works' as an integrated product.
quotes.key_insight: When evaluating hardware products, the integrated software experience matters as much as the underlying technology, and SLA's superior print quality and speed make it the better choice for those seeking polished results over raw hackability.

quotes.quote: It infuriates me to no end that consumers have an option of even buying a $400 computer that will give them such a terrible experience.
quotes.summary: Frazelle uses her mother's frustrating experience with cheap, underpowered Windows machines to argue that manufacturers shouldn't sell computers with specs so low they provide terrible user experiences. She contrasts this with Apple's approach of maintaining minimum quality standards, then pivots to an optimistic vision: emerging technologies like process migration and 'teleforking' could let even low-spec machines offload computation to remote resources seamlessly. The future of computing should make powerful compute readily available to everyone, not just those who can afford premium hardware.
quotes.key_insight: The solution to the epidemic of underpowered consumer computers isn't just better hardware standards—it's making compute a seamlessly shareable, distributed resource so local specs become irrelevant.

quotes.quote: If we know that the pixel density of televisions is only going to increase over time, causing streaming services to need more bandwidth, why are we not seeing a bunch of fiber being laid down or other innovations to get faster internet to the mass market?
quotes.summary: As display technology advances from HD to 4K to 8K, bandwidth requirements are growing exponentially—8K streaming requires 100mbps while average US speeds barely reach 96mbps. The 'last mile' problem makes fiber deployment prohibitively expensive, as Google Fiber discovered. Satellite internet from companies like Starlink and Astranis may finally solve the connectivity gap, delivering gigabit speeds without the infrastructure challenges of laying physical cable to every home.
quotes.key_insight: Display technology is advancing faster than internet infrastructure can support it, and satellite internet may be the only economically viable path to universal high-bandwidth access.

quotes.quote: Time is one of the most valuable resources in the world. If there was something you could do to free more time for yourself, why wouldn't you?
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle argues that automation is fundamentally about reclaiming time by transferring repetitive manual tasks to scripts and services. She presents a simple equation: time gained equals time doing the task manually minus time to automate it. The post covers her personal automations (employee onboarding in Rust, Gmail filters, newsletter management) while celebrating how consumer tools like Apple Shortcuts, home automation, and IFTTT are democratizing automation patterns for non-programmers.
quotes.key_insight: Automation is fundamentally a time arbitrage calculation, and consumer tools are finally democratizing this productivity superpower beyond programmers.

quotes.quote: At this point in history, you could measure the speed of a storage algorithm by the distance the tape traveled.
quotes.summary: This post traces the evolution of data storage from 1951's UNISERVO tape drives through modern NVMe and emerging technologies like HAMR and storage class memory. Using a single byte (the letter 'j') as a narrative thread, Frazelle explains how each storage medium physically represents 0s and 1s—from holes in paper cards to magnetic polarities to trapped electrons in floating gates. The piece concludes by examining cutting-edge technologies like phase change memory, helium-filled drives, and heat-assisted magnetic recording that will define storage's future.
quotes.key_insight: While storage technology has advanced exponentially in speed and density over 70 years, the fundamental abstraction remains unchanged: everything reduces to representing and persisting 0s and 1s through whatever physical mechanism the era's technology enables.

quotes.quote: Most companies become 'carbon neutral' by investing in offsets that primarily avoid emissions, such as paying folks to not cut down trees or buying RECs. These offsets do not actually remove the carbon that they are emitting.
quotes.summary: This post provides a comprehensive technical breakdown of data center power efficiency, explaining the differences between 'carbon neutral,' 'net zero,' and 'carbon negative' designations while examining how hyperscalers like Google, Facebook, and Microsoft optimize power usage. Frazelle dives deep into the engineering trade-offs of power distribution systems, comparing rack-level DC bus bars to individual AC power supplies, and explains how the Open Compute Project's designs achieve significant efficiency gains. The post argues that wider adoption of rack-scale architectures could reduce wasted carbon emissions across the industry, not just at hyperscale companies.
quotes.key_insight: Rack-level power architectures with DC bus bars can achieve 8-12% efficiency gains over commodity servers, and broader adoption of Open Compute designs would significantly reduce the tech industry's carbon footprint beyond just hyperscalers.

quotes.quote: Hyperscalers like Facebook, Google, and Microsoft have what I like to call 'infrastructure privilege' since they long ago decided they could build their own hardware and software to fulfill their needs better than commodity vendors.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle announces the founding of Oxide Computer Company alongside Steve Tuck and Bryan Cantrill, a venture aimed at building integrated hardware and software for on-premises infrastructure. After extensive conversations with companies running their own infrastructure, she identified a significant pain point: organizations with legitimate needs for on-premises computing (security, latency, economics) have been neglected by existing vendors. Oxide aims to bring the 'infrastructure privilege' that hyperscalers like Google and Facebook enjoy to everyone else, through better hardware-software integration, power distribution, and density.
quotes.key_insight: The gap between hyperscaler infrastructure capabilities and what's available to everyone else represents a massive opportunity to bring integrated, purpose-built hardware and software to organizations with legitimate on-premises needs.

quotes.quote: If you've ever used a Supermicro server I probably don't need to tell you that it's a piece of shit running a 2.6 linux kernel on the BMC.
quotes.summary: Frazelle recounts her experience at the Open Source Firmware Conference hackathon, where she attempted to replace proprietary BMC firmware with OpenBMC on a Supermicro motherboard. The project hit numerous obstacles including SPI flash contention issues, write speed problems, and debugging without a serial console. Despite not achieving a fully working open source BMC by the end, she frames the failures as valuable learning experiences about low-level hardware debugging.
quotes.key_insight: The messy, failure-prone process of hacking on open source firmware—complete with desoldering chips and debugging SPI flash timing issues—is ultimately more educational and rewarding than a smooth success would have been.

quotes.quote: Transactional memory removes the possibility of getting a deadlock and replaces it with what is known as a livelock.
quotes.summary: Frazelle uses transactional memory (specifically Intel's TSX) as a case study of how tech hype can lead to flawed implementations. She explains how transactional memory trades deadlocks for livelocks and creates security vulnerabilities that enabled attacks like DrK to break KASLR without generating page faults. The post traces how the transactional memory hype originated in academia around 2008, was implemented by Intel in 2012 despite practitioner warnings, and reflects on how hype propagation channels have evolved from academic journals to social media.
quotes.key_insight: Tech hype that originates in academia and ignores practitioner warnings can lead to flawed implementations with real security consequences, as demonstrated by Intel's TSX enabling side-channel attacks that practitioners predicted would be problematic.

quotes.quote: Intel's implementation of TSX behaves in such a way that when a transaction aborts due to a hardware exception, it does not fire typical exceptions. Instead, it invokes a user-specified abort handler without informing the underlying OS. This seems like it might lead to some really bad behavior… we should probably know wtf is going on in our system at any given point in time.

quotes.quote: There is a lot of hype in our field and little truth behind the hype.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle offers business executives a reality check on Kubernetes adoption, cutting through the hype to explain the significant challenges organizations face. She covers the risks of using Kubernetes for stateful data, the security dangers of exposed dashboards, the operational pain of upgrades that consistently break production, and the steep learning curve that leads to outages. Even managed Kubernetes offerings don't eliminate these problems—they still require deep expertise and carry their own isolation and security risks.
quotes.key_insight: Kubernetes can deliver real benefits, but executives need to understand the significant operational complexity, security risks, and learning curve costs before adopting it—managed offerings don't eliminate these challenges, they just shift some of the burden.

quotes.quote: No longer would my scars from iptables bleed with another bug!
quotes.summary: This foreword for the 'Linux Observability with BPF' book explains why BPF (Berkeley Packet Filters) and XDP (Express Data Path) represent a significant advancement in Linux kernel capabilities. Drawing from her experience as a Docker core maintainer dealing with iptables pain points, Frazelle explains how BPF solves real problems in container networking, system tracing, and DDoS mitigation. She emphasizes that BPF enables developers to trace syscalls, build custom tools, and prove performance issues with hard data, all while being production-ready due to its ability to filter and aggregate at the source of events.
quotes.key_insight: BPF represents a paradigm shift in Linux observability and networking by moving filtering and tracing into the kernel itself, enabling production-grade tooling that was previously impossible with tools like iptables.

quotes.quote: It's counter-intuitive that the code that we have the least visibility into has the most privileges. This is what open source firmware is aiming to fix.
quotes.summary: The post examines the security implications of proprietary firmware running at the most privileged levels of computing hardware (Rings -2 and -3), which paradoxically have the least visibility despite having more access than the operating system or hypervisor. Frazelle explains how the Intel Management Engine and UEFI kernel contain hidden kernels with their own networking stacks and web servers, creating massive attack surfaces. She advocates for open source firmware initiatives like linuxboot, coreboot, and the NERF stack as solutions to improve security through transparency and reduced complexity.
quotes.key_insight: True hardware security is impossible to achieve or verify when the most privileged code in our systems remains proprietary and invisible, making open source firmware essential rather than optional.

quotes.quote: Usually when I have a complex bot I end up writing a lot of 'glue code' to combine a few different APIs and get the information I want.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle tests Transposit, a SQL interface for APIs, by rebuilding her 'Gitable' bot that syncs GitHub issues to Airtable. She walks through replacing complex 'glue code' and N+1 query problems with simple SQL joins across APIs. Despite attempting to break the platform with her 'weirdest' bot, the tool handles the complexity gracefully, including pagination and API rate limiting.
quotes.key_insight: Complex API integration code with N+1 problems and pagination logic can be dramatically simplified using a SQL abstraction layer that handles the optimization automatically.

quotes.quote: There are devastating consequences to taking actions based on fear and pride; we hope this set of questions enables taking action based on growth, humility, inclusion, and soulful reflection.
quotes.summary: The post proposes a 'Leadership CI' framework—a set of self-assessment questions leaders should ask themselves before making decisions, analogous to how software uses continuous integration tests before merging code. Co-authored with Kathy Simpson, it argues that leaders need pre-established checks to avoid decisions driven by fear and pride, instead favoring growth, humility, and inclusion. The framework includes questions about ego, transparency, curiosity, manipulation, and whether you're empowering your team or creating an echo chamber.
quotes.key_insight: Just as software engineers use CI to catch bugs before they ship, leaders should use a personal checklist of introspective questions to catch ego-driven or fear-based decisions before they cause harm.

quotes.summary: Frazelle reflects on spending a day shadowing investigative journalist Jeff Kao and discovering parallels between journalism and her technical background. She explores how software engineering tools like diffs and version control could serve as mechanisms for transparency and truth-finding in journalism and governance. The post draws connections between text comparison techniques used in investigative pieces (like uncovering fake net neutrality comments) and the everyday tools of source control.

quotes.quote: All of the hardware engineers either laugh or are horrified and the resounding reaction is 'you'd be crazy to think hardware was ever intended to be used for isolating multiple users safely.'
quotes.summary: Frazelle applies Conway's Law to the software stack and identifies a critical problem: insufficient communication between hardware, firmware, kernel, and application layers leads to dangerous security assumptions. She provides examples from hardware engineers who never intended chips for multi-tenant isolation (leading to Spectre/Meltdown), firmware complexity that frustrates kernel developers, and Kubernetes being misused for multi-tenant isolation despite not being designed for it. The post questions how the industry can solve this fundamental disconnect between stack layers.
quotes.key_insight: The entire cloud computing industry is built on dangerous assumptions because different layers of the stack—from hardware to orchestration—were designed in isolation without understanding how they would be composed for multi-tenant security.

quotes.quote: I think anyone is capable of doing or learning anything, they just need the right motivation and to believe in themselves.
quotes.summary: Frazelle uses her exploration of RISC-V as a case study for how she learns new technical topics. Rather than diving straight into RISC-V specifics, she recursively digs into foundational concepts like instruction set architectures, examining RISC, CISC, VLIW, and other ISA types first. She reviews the Berkeley RISC-V design paper, highlighting why existing proprietary ISAs like x86 (with ~2,500 instructions) and ARM were unsuitable, and why an open, simpler architecture was needed. The post deliberately ends before covering RISC-V's actual design to illustrate that deep learning requires extensive foundational exploration.
quotes.key_insight: Deep technical learning requires embracing a recursive process of exploring foundational concepts before tackling your actual goal, treating it like a choose-your-own-adventure game where you dig into whatever sparks curiosity.

quotes.quote: This is my learning technique. I bounce from one thing to another, recursively digging deeper as I learn more.

quotes.quote: The 'N+1 shithead problem' occurs when you are demotivated by seeing people who are a level above you behave poorly, or more bluntly when they behave like a shithead.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle defines what a distinguished engineer should embody after experiencing the 'N+1 shithead problem' - being demotivated by seeing senior engineers behave poorly. She outlines a comprehensive framework covering technical leadership qualities like empowering others, giving constructive criticism, having opinions loosely held, and prioritizing shipping. The post emphasizes that distinguished engineers must also engage with external communities, remain humble, and ultimately demonstrate these values through actions rather than words.
quotes.key_insight: A distinguished engineer's value lies not in technical brilliance alone, but in their ability to empower others, remain humble, and model the behavior that makes junior engineers want to reach that level.

The post emphasizes that distinguished engineers must also engage with external communities, remain humble, and ultimately demonstrate these values through actions rather than words.
quotes.key_insight: A distinguished engineer's value lies not in technical brilliance alone, but in their ability to empower others, remain humble, and model the behavior that makes junior engineers want to reach that level.

quotes.quote: Honestly, unikernels replacing all the crap firmware in the world would be a huge win.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle recaps her recent adventures exploring cutting-edge open source hardware and firmware initiatives, from seeing unikernels boot on RISC-V at Cambridge to witnessing Facebook's open firmware efforts at Open Compute Summit. She highlights the exciting progress in replacing proprietary firmware with open alternatives like LinuxBoot and Coreboot, while critiquing Intel's Boot Guard for locking users out of their own hardware. The post serves as a travelogue through the frontier of open hardware innovation during her time between jobs.
quotes.key_insight: Hardware ownership should include firmware ownership, and the open firmware movement is making real progress despite vendor lock-in mechanisms like Intel's Boot Guard.

quotes.quote: I became better at reading people after having a few really bad experiences where I should have known better than to trust someone.
quotes.summary: Frazelle reflects on trust and integrity in leadership after spending time observing the finance industry. She argues that transparency and trust go hand-in-hand, and leaders must earn trust through a track record of doing the right thing rather than assuming it will be given. She criticizes leaders who weaponize information asymmetry against their reports, and emphasizes that unlike respect, trust starts at zero and must be actively built.
quotes.key_insight: Trust is not given but earned through consistent integrity, and leaders who default to secrecy over transparency are often weaponizing information asymmetry against their own teams.

quotes.quote: I believe that most titles are bullshit, climbing a career ladder is bullshit, what really matters is what you do and what impact you have.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle documents her experiences shadowing professionals in government (Pentagon/US Digital Service) and medicine (surgical residency) to compare their organizational cultures with tech. She finds that both fields have hierarchical structures with meaningful lessons: military badges reward actual accomplishments rather than political climbing, and attending physicians work collaboratively with residents rather than dictating from above. She contrasts these healthier dynamics with tech's dysfunction around meaningless titles and managers who hoard technical decisions instead of empowering their teams.
quotes.key_insight: Functional hierarchies in government and medicine reward actual accomplishments and enable collaborative leadership, while tech often conflates titles with power and promotes managers who hoard decisions instead of empowering teams.

quotes.quote: I call this genre of books 'romanticized tech' because of the way tech is portrayed in them in a very idealistic and whimsical way. It's nice to pick up one of these if you are feeling very 'Black Mirror' to remember why you might have even started in this field.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle shares her curated reading list divided into 'romanticized tech' books that capture the idealistic spirit of computing history, and non-fiction spanning leadership, science, and self-improvement. The romanticized tech section features books about Data General, CERN, Commodore, and the origins of the Internet that remind readers why they entered the field. The non-fiction recommendations range from technical resources like Google's datacenter design to management guides and physics texts, reflecting her diverse interests in leadership, mathematics, and particle physics.
quotes.key_insight: Reading romanticized accounts of computing history can rekindle the passion and idealism that originally drew engineers to the field, serving as a counterbalance to tech cynicism.

quotes.quote: I like to think that I write how I speak. This was not always taken well, however. I tend to be a bit of a sarcastic troll.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle shares her college entrance essay to encourage others who may have been criticized for their unconventional writing style. She recounts how her satirical senior thesis about the moon landing being fake earned her first D, but she refused to compromise her authentic voice. The essay itself, 'Pharmy Tales,' is a collection of humorous vignettes from her high school job at a pharmacy, showcasing her natural storytelling ability and sardonic wit.
quotes.key_insight: Authentic self-expression and unconventional thinking, even when criticized by authority figures, can be a strength rather than a liability.

quotes.quote: Having this context makes the problems that arise when you try to do code execution inside an enclave seem like a forgivable sin.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle revisits her earlier critique of Intel SGX after learning it was originally designed for DRM, not as a general execution environment. She acknowledges the HAVEN paper's novelty in repurposing enclaves for code execution and praises Joanna Rutkowska's thoughtful approach to SGX tradeoffs at Golem. The post emphasizes the importance of understanding a technology's original design intent before judging its limitations, and reflects on how users inevitably push technologies beyond their intended purposes.
quotes.key_insight: Technologies should be evaluated in the context of their original design intent, and what appears to be a flaw may simply be users pushing the technology into use cases it was never built to handle.

quotes.quote: I am going to take the time right now to make one thing very clear, VERY CLEAR, listen closely: LD_PRELOAD IS NOT A FLAG. It is an environment variable. Wake up sheeple!
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle professes her love for LD_PRELOAD, an environment variable that allows overriding library symbols by loading custom shared objects before any other library. She clarifies that LD_PRELOAD is NOT a linker flag (despite common confusion), then showcases creative uses collected from Twitter, including hacking Quake 3, bypassing Dropbox filesystem restrictions, and reproducing SSH key vulnerabilities. The post culminates in her satirical 'Cloud Native Syscalls' project that uses LD_PRELOAD to forward all syscalls to a remote cloud daemon.
quotes.key_insight: LD_PRELOAD is an incredibly powerful Linux mechanism for intercepting and replacing library functions, enabling everything from debugging and security research to creative hacks and satirical cloud architectures.

quotes.quote: LD_PRELOAD allows you to override symbols in any library by specifying your new function in a shared object.

quotes.quote: In today's age, at large companies, most engineers take jobs where they work on one teeny aspect of a machine or website or app. Sometimes they are not even aware of the larger goal or vision but just their own little world.
quotes.summary: Frazelle argues that the end of Moore's Law, rather than being a crisis, opens up a new golden age for computer architecture and passionate building. Drawing inspiration from 'The Soul of a New Machine' and the garage-computer-building era, she contrasts the soul-driven work of small, mission-aligned teams with today's fragmented corporate engineering roles. She sees democratization of hardware design through open source tools as enabling a return to that era of building with passion and purpose.
quotes.key_insight: The end of Moore's Law combined with open source hardware tools creates an opportunity to return to building computers with soul—small teams driven by passion and accomplishment rather than corporate fragmentation.

quotes.quote: So in between bouncing back and forth reading about various forms of firmware and how shitty and sketchy closed source firmware is, I read the book Bad Blood... so everything from here on out is with 'paranoid as fuck' goggles on because I was shook.
quotes.summary: Frazelle documents her deep dive into firmware and hardware security during her unemployment, exploring baseboard management controllers (BMC), Intel Management Engine, and Intel SGX. She traces her path from openbmc and u-bmc projects through the security vulnerabilities of closed-source firmware, ultimately landing on SGX's extensive attack surface including Foreshadow and cache attacks. The post reveals how technologies marketed as security features often contain hidden complexities, licensing restrictions, and fundamental vulnerabilities that undermine their stated purpose.
quotes.key_insight: Hardware security features like Intel SGX are often undermined by fundamental architectural vulnerabilities and corporate licensing schemes, making their security promises more marketing than reality.

quotes.quote: If one layer of isolation is compromised, rely on another layer of isolation entirely. If two layers are compromised then we at least tried our best…
quotes.summary: This design document outlines a secure multi-tenant container orchestrator for running untrusted third-party code in isolation. Frazelle argues for using Firecracker VMs instead of plain containers to allow full syscall access while maintaining security through multiple isolation layers. The design emphasizes defense in depth with locked-down host OSes, network deny-all defaults, eBPF monitoring, and explicit rejection of Kubernetes due to its complexity and large attack surface.
quotes.key_insight: True multi-tenant security for arbitrary code execution requires defense in depth with VM-level isolation, and Kubernetes' complexity makes it unsuitable for high-security multi-tenancy despite its popularity.

quotes.quote: We should have some ways of coupling programs like garden hose--screw in another segment when it becomes necessary to massage data in another way.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle celebrates the Unix pipe as a brilliant piece of software design that embodies the Unix philosophy of making programs that do one thing well and can be combined with others. She traces the concept back to Doug McIlroy's 1964 memo proposing ways to 'couple programs like garden hose.' The post argues that this design pattern enables creativity and extensibility by allowing simple tools to become building blocks for solving complex problems the original authors never anticipated.
quotes.key_insight: The Unix pipe represents brilliant software design because it allows simple, single-purpose programs to become building blocks for solving complex problems their original authors never imagined.

quotes.summary: This post walks through the complete lifecycle of a GitHub Action, from workflow file configuration to Docker container execution. Using a practical example of automatically deleting merged PR branches, Frazelle explains how GitHub triggers events, resolves action dependencies, builds Docker images, and executes scripts. The tutorial demystifies the Actions system by showing each component: workflow files, Dockerfiles, and the bash scripts that do the actual work.
quotes.key_insight: GitHub Actions is essentially 'scriptable GitHub' where Docker containers run in response to repository events, with the workflow file orchestrating the trigger conditions and the Dockerfile defining the execution environment.

quotes.quote: I have realized recently that a lot of people think I am just a shill for Kubernetes and I am not.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle pushes back against the assumption that she's a Kubernetes advocate, clarifying that the problems she's written about are often specific to Kubernetes' design choices rather than universal container orchestration challenges. She argues that many teams could build simpler, custom orchestrators using containerd directly, and that the truly hard problems (like unprivileged image building) exist independent of Kubernetes. The post is a call for pragmatism over hype-driven technology choices.
quotes.key_insight: Kubernetes complexity is often unnecessary—choose the simplest tool that solves your actual problem rather than following industry hype.

The post includes a complete walkthrough of setting up a Wireguard connection using the demo server, with an update noting that Tailscale now offers an easier cross-platform solution using a Go implementation.
quotes.key_insight: Containers can encapsulate and manage even kernel-level software like Wireguard, maintaining a clean host system while still gaining full functionality.

quotes.quote: Greetings. Reporting for duty. Literally I am the person who can do that. I was the person who did do that.
quotes.summary: Frazelle pushes back against marketing claims that existing container security mechanisms are inadequate, pointing out that she personally implemented Docker's default Seccomp and Apparmor profiles which provide multiple overlapping layers of protection. She argues that gvisor's marketing dismisses this work while spreading FUD, and that large organizations suffer from 'not invented here' syndrome combined with echo chambers that prevent them from recognizing external contributions. The post defends the extensive testing and thoughtful defaults built into container runtimes while questioning whether gvisor actually provides better security given its operational complexity and larger syscall surface.
quotes.key_insight: Marketing hype from large organizations often dismisses existing security work without proper research, creating echo chambers that spread FUD rather than building on the defense-in-depth mechanisms already implemented in container runtimes.

quotes.quote: Kubernetes is the new kernel. We can refer to it as a 'cluster kernel' versus the typical operating system kernel.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle argues that achieving hard multi-tenancy in Kubernetes requires more than one layer of security, drawing parallels to how operating system kernels handle privilege separation. She proposes applying 'intra-kernel isolation' to Kubernetes, where each namespace gets its own nested Kubernetes system services (api-server, kube-proxy, etc.), so that exploiting one tenant's services doesn't compromise the entire cluster. The post outlines how combining namespace isolation with node-level resource control—or alternatively using VM-based container isolation—can provide defense in depth against both API vulnerabilities and container breakouts.
quotes.key_insight: Hard multi-tenancy in Kubernetes requires treating it like an operating system kernel and applying proven intra-kernel isolation techniques, giving each tenant their own nested control plane rather than relying on a single shared system that can be fully compromised by one exploit.

quotes.quote: Bullshit, if it was easy someone would have done it already. I've wanted this for years. Please pass the ranch dressing.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle documents her journey to create 'img', an unprivileged container image builder for Kubernetes, after a challenge from a coworker who claimed it couldn't be done. She builds on prior art from Docker colleagues and the buildkit project, then systematically works through the technical obstacles of running unprivileged builds inside containers—requiring patches to runc, Docker, and Kubernetes itself. The post demonstrates how achieving seemingly simple goals in the container ecosystem often requires deep yak-shaving across multiple layers of the stack.
quotes.key_insight: Building unprivileged container images in Kubernetes is achievable but requires coordinated patches across multiple layers of the container stack—from runc to Docker to Kubernetes—demonstrating how deeply intertwined these systems have become.

quotes.quote: Right, so I want to do this with *all* languages… but what I also hate is installing shit on my host. Ew, we have containers for those silly things.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle takes a Cloudflare blog post about using BINFMT_MISC to script in Go and extends it to run any language through containers. She combines her binctr project (which embeds container images into static binaries) with BINFMT_MISC to execute Common Lisp scripts in isolated containers without installing anything on the host. The result is a proof-of-concept where any script can be 'dot slashed' and run in its own container environment.
quotes.key_insight: By combining kernel-level binary format registration with self-contained container binaries, you can create a system where any programming language can be executed as a script without polluting your host with dependencies.

quotes.quote: I am super excited about Windows Subsystem for Linux. It is one of the coolest pieces of tech I've seen since I started using Docker.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle explores Windows Subsystem for Linux (WSL) after joining Microsoft, explaining how it works at a technical level including Pico processes, system call translation, and the ability to run Linux binaries natively without a VM. She walks through the architecture where WSL kernel drivers translate Linux syscalls to Windows NT APIs, discusses practical features like launching Windows executables from WSL and running X applications, and shares pain points around copy/paste, file performance, and filesystem interoperability. The post also covers how to set up a Windows machine reproducibly using Boxstarter.
quotes.key_insight: Windows Subsystem for Linux represents a remarkable engineering achievement where Microsoft engineers reimplemented Linux syscalls from scratch, creating a native Linux compatibility layer that rivals containerization in its technical elegance.

quotes.quote: There is a point at which better security comes at the expense of convenience. This needs to stop happening. Stop compromising convenience for security.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle argues that security tools have historically been too difficult to use, causing users to disable them entirely. She advocates for finding the right balance between security and usability through collaboration between security and software engineers. Using Docker's default seccomp and AppArmor profiles as examples of security made invisible to users, she proposes a future where programming languages generate seccomp filters at build-time, allowing binaries to sandbox themselves automatically.
quotes.key_insight: The best security is invisible security—enabled by default, requiring no user configuration, and generated deterministically at build-time rather than through error-prone runtime profiling.

quotes.quote: Linux namespaces are one of the primitives that make up what is known as a 'container.' They control what a process can see. Cgroups, the other main ingredient of 'containers', control what a process can use.
quotes.summary: This post explains two Linux kernel objects that aren't namespaced in containers: time and the kernel keyring. Frazelle demonstrates that changing time inside a container affects the host system, and that keys stored in the kernel keyring by one container are visible to all other containers on the same host. She emphasizes that Docker's default seccomp profiles block these potentially dangerous operations, reinforcing that namespaces and cgroups alone don't provide complete container isolation.
quotes.key_insight: Containers provide incomplete isolation by default because critical kernel objects like time and the keyring aren't namespaced, making seccomp profiles and capability restrictions essential for security.

quotes.quote: A 'container' is just a term people use to describe a combination of Linux namespaces and cgroups. Linux namespaces and cgroups ARE first class objects. NOT containers.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle argues that containers are fundamentally different from Solaris Zones, BSD Jails, and VMs because containers are not first-class objects—they're built from Linux primitives (namespaces and cgroups) rather than being designed as top-level isolation mechanisms. This architectural difference gives containers unique flexibility, like sharing namespaces between processes, but also introduces complexity that leads to security bugs. She advocates for improving container security through hardening rather than abandoning them for alternatives that serve different use cases.
quotes.key_insight: Containers' flexibility and security trade-offs stem from being assembled from primitives rather than designed as first-class isolation units, making them fundamentally different tools than VMs, Zones, or Jails—not inferior ones.

quotes.quote: So just remember that before you get your panties in a knot on HackerNews because you live and die by Xmonad (I live and die by i3, fight me).
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle describes her quest to build the ultimate Linux desktop experience by modifying CoreOS Container Linux (a server OS) to run on her laptop with graphics drivers, then running all applications in rootless containers. She hacked Gentoo-based ebuilds to add Intel graphics support, set up auto-updates via an Omaha server, and leveraged the read-only /usr filesystem to enforce containerization of everything. The setup represents her vision of a secure, automatically-updating desktop where even the container runtime doesn't require root privileges.
quotes.key_insight: A truly secure containerized desktop requires not just running apps in containers, but ensuring the container runtime itself doesn't need root privileges, combined with an immutable base OS that enforces containerization by design.

quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle documents the chaos that ensued when she changed her GitHub username from 'jfrazelle' to 'jessfraz' to match her other online handles. What started as a simple username swap cascaded into broken Jenkins builds, failed Go imports across 50+ repositories, and potential breakage for other developers who depended on her repos. She walks through her systematic approach to fixing everything using sed, vim's argdo command, and pull requests, while maintaining her characteristic humor about self-inflicted infrastructure pain.
quotes.key_insight: Infrastructure changes that seem simple can cascade into unexpected breakage across interconnected systems, but systematic scripting and a tolerance for self-inflicted pain can get you through.

quotes.quote: Getting paid to work on open source is a rare and wonderful opportunity, but you should not have to give up your passion in the process. Your passion should be why companies want to pay you.
quotes.summary: Frazelle examines the tension between personal passion for open source projects and corporate interests when getting paid to maintain them. Drawing from her experience on Docker's core team, she argues that maintainers can and should stand up for project integrity while keeping their jobs. The post outlines practical lessons for companies backing open source projects, emphasizing that community trust depends on everyone playing by the same rules regardless of employment status.
quotes.key_insight: Corporate-backed open source succeeds when companies recognize that maintainers' passion and loyalty to the project must come before company interests, and when everyone—employees and external contributors alike—plays by the same rules.

quotes.quote: Which got me wondering about my favorite subject ever, The Art of Closing.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle uses BigQuery to analyze GitHub Archive data, examining pull request statistics across popular repositories in 2015. She calculates merge ratios for top projects by unique contributors, finding that Docker had a 75.79% merge rate. The analysis culminates in comparing the average size of merged vs. closed pull requests to test the hypothesis that smaller PRs are more likely to be merged.
quotes.key_insight: Data from GitHub's top repositories confirms the conventional wisdom that smaller pull requests are significantly more likely to be merged than larger ones.

quotes.quote: Varnish rags are flammable and in combination with the Arizona heat this caused spontaneous combustion. I kid you not.
quotes.summary: Frazelle shares a childhood memory from 1995 Phoenix when varnish rags left by painters spontaneously combusted in the extreme Arizona heat, causing a house fire that destroyed much of her family's home. The post reflects on how losing material possessions—including her beloved teddy bears—taught her an early lesson about what truly matters. She closes with an eerie coincidence: a fire-scorched page of the Ten Commandments from discarded Sunday school books landed at their front door and remains framed in her parents' home today.
quotes.key_insight: A childhood house fire taught the valuable lesson that material possessions are ultimately replaceable, but relationships with people are what truly matter.

quotes.quote: Rule #1 of open-source: no is temporary, yes is forever.
quotes.summary: Being an open source maintainer requires learning to say 'no' to patches, which is one of the hardest parts of the job. The post outlines several techniques for closing pull requests gracefully while minimizing contributor frustration, including the 'ego stroke and close,' closing early before contributors invest more work, and 'carrying' patches to completion when contributors disappear. Frazelle emphasizes that maintainers deal with an information asymmetry where contributors treat their PRs as pets while maintainers must manage them as cattle.
quotes.key_insight: Closing pull requests is an unavoidable skill for maintainers, and doing it with empathy and clear communication—while being decisive and timely—minimizes frustration for everyone involved.

quotes.quote: One of the key differences between how you run Chrome and how you run a container are the privileges used. Chrome runs as your own unprivileged user. Most containers (be it docker, runc, or rkt) run as root.
quotes.summary: The post argues that while containers and sandboxes use the same Linux primitives (namespaces, seccomp, AppArmor), containers aren't true sandboxes primarily because they require root privileges to create and run. By leveraging user namespaces, it's possible to create containers as an unprivileged user, though cgroups remain a challenge. The author presents binctr as a proof of concept and advocates for evolving containers toward real sandbox isolation.
quotes.key_insight: True container sandboxing requires running without root privileges, which user namespaces can enable, but the real security value comes from application-specific profiles rather than generic container configurations.

quotes.quote: Frankenkernels are not to be trusted. This is not to say it won't work, hey it might work great, but it's not to be trusted.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle provides an unfiltered evaluation of Docker's storage drivers (graphdrivers), examining the tradeoffs of each option. She advocates for Overlay as her personal choice despite some kernel bugs, dismisses Devicemapper as unreliable outside RedHat, and warns that each driver has significant setup requirements or limitations. The post cuts through marketing to give practitioners real-world guidance on which storage backend to choose.
quotes.key_insight: Every Docker storage driver has significant tradeoffs, but Overlay with a modern kernel is the most practical choice for most users, while Devicemapper should be avoided unless you're on RedHat.

quotes.quote: This is so cool I can hardly stand it.
quotes.summary: Docker 1.10 introduced the ability to assign specific IP addresses to containers through libnetwork, eliminating the need for hacky workarounds like pipework or custom bash scripts. This feature allows users with dedicated IP blocks to easily assign public IPs directly to containers with simple commands. Jess demonstrates the straightforward three-step process: create a network with your subnet, run a container with a specific IP, and access it publicly.
quotes.key_insight: Native IP assignment in Docker 1.10 transforms a previously hacky container networking task into a simple three-command workflow.

quotes.quote: This is the awesome thing about runc. You, the user, have all the control.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle documents her migration from Docker to runc for running desktop applications in containers, motivated by the desire for fine-grained control over user namespace mappings. She needed custom gid mappings to access audio and video devices while still benefiting from user namespace isolation. To facilitate this workflow, she built three tools: Riddler (converts Docker containers to OCI specs), Netns (provides bridge networking for runc containers), and Magneto (displays container stats).
quotes.key_insight: When higher-level container tools don't expose the low-level controls you need, dropping down to runc gives you complete power over namespace mappings and container configuration.

quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle demonstrates how to run desktop applications and Docker-in-Docker using Docker's new user namespace support coming in version 1.10. She walks through practical examples including Chrome, the notify-osd daemon with irssi IRC client integration, and explains how to achieve Docker-in-Docker without the --privileged flag by carefully selecting only the necessary capabilities (CAP_SYS_ADMIN and CAP_NET_ADMIN) and adjusting seccomp/apparmor profiles.
quotes.key_insight: The --privileged flag is a sledgehammer when you usually only need a scalpel—understanding which specific Linux capabilities you actually need enables more secure container configurations.

quotes.quote: So this is not the most sane thing in the world, I even tried in the process to create a bash script that takes the output from strace, collects the syscalls, and generates a profile. But like all tools of this sort it missed some, well to be exact it missed 6.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle explains how to create custom seccomp profiles for Docker containers, walking through her experience generating a profile for Chrome by running it under strace for an entire day. She demonstrates that even with automated tools, the resulting profile missed 6 critical syscalls that required manual debugging and domain knowledge to identify. The post serves as both a tutorial and an argument for why Docker needed to ship sane default seccomp profiles that protect users without requiring this painful manual process.
quotes.key_insight: Good security defaults matter because the manual alternative of creating custom seccomp profiles requires deep systems knowledge and hours of painful debugging that no normal user will actually do.

quotes.quote: A reverse VPN is for exposing things from your home network into the public. Why? Well for one, you shouldn't want to expose your home network to the world. There are a lot of risks in doing that. A reverse VPN allows you to securely control what you are exposing.
quotes.summary: The post explains how to set up a reverse VPN to securely expose services from a home network to the public internet, rather than the traditional use of VPNs for accessing office networks remotely. Jess walks through the complete Docker-based setup using OpenVPN, from generating certificates on a cloud instance to configuring the client on a home NAS. The key insight is using Docker's network namespace sharing to route specific containers through the VPN tunnel, giving fine-grained control over what gets exposed.
quotes.key_insight: Docker's network namespace sharing combined with a reverse VPN creates a secure, granular way to expose specific home services to the internet without opening up your entire network.

quotes.quote: It's actually a lot simplier than you would think. But it involves dealing with some unsavory things such as iptables.
quotes.summary: This technical tutorial demonstrates how to route all host traffic through a Tor Docker container using iptables rules. Frazelle provides a straightforward approach: run her Tor image with host networking, then apply iptables NAT and filter rules to redirect all TCP traffic through Tor's transparent proxy port while allowing local network exceptions. The post acknowledges the tradeoff of using --net host but argues it's pragmatic given the goal of routing all traffic.
quotes.key_insight: Containerizing Tor and using host networking with iptables provides a clean, reproducible way to route all system traffic through the Tor network without complex Docker networking workarounds.

quotes.quote: This blog post is going to be about how I manage ~50 servers but don't do anything at all.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle explains how she manages Docker's testing infrastructure of ~50 servers as a part-time responsibility while working as a core maintainer. The infrastructure tests Docker across 6 storage drivers, multiple Linux distros, various kernel versions, and Windows clients, processing hundreds of builds weekly. By running everything in Docker containers—Jenkins, Consul, IRC bots, webhook services—and using tools like Consul for monitoring, the system essentially runs itself, allowing her to focus on her primary engineering work.
quotes.key_insight: Running your entire testing infrastructure in the same technology you're building creates a virtuous cycle where dogfooding catches bugs early while automation keeps operational overhead minimal.

quotes.quote: I use Docker to run all the desktop apps on my computers.
quotes.summary: Jess Frazelle demonstrates an unconventional use of Docker: running desktop applications in containers on Linux. Motivated by the desire for OS X-style application sandboxing and clean uninstalls, she shows how to containerize everything from text-based apps (IRC, email, Twitter clients) to full GUI applications (Chrome, Spotify, Skype, Tor Browser) by mounting the X11 socket. This approach provides resource control (CPU/memory limits), application isolation, and the satisfaction of knowing deleted apps leave no traces behind.
quotes.key_insight: Docker containers aren't just for production deployments—they can sandbox desktop applications, giving you resource control and clean isolation that traditional package managers can't provide.

quotes.quote: I hate installing things on my host and the files getting everywhere. I wanted the ability to delete an app and know it is gone fully without some random file hanging around.

This approach provides resource control (CPU/memory limits), application isolation, and the satisfaction of knowing deleted apps leave no traces behind.
quotes.key_insight: Docker containers aren't just for production deployments—they can sandbox desktop applications, giving you resource control and clean isolation that traditional package managers can't provide.

quotes.summary: This tutorial walks through the complete process of installing Debian Linux on a MacBook Pro Retina, covering boot manager setup with rEFInd, disk partitioning, building a custom 3.17.x kernel for thunderbolt hotplugging support, and configuring drivers for wifi, graphics, and touchpad. Frazelle documents the numerous pain points and workarounds required, from dealing with EFI boot quirks to fixing CD-ROM mount errors during installation. The post reflects her frustration with macOS and her determination to run Linux as her primary OS, even sharing her custom kernel configs and shell scripts for backlight control.
quotes.key_insight: Running Linux on Mac hardware requires significant effort including custom kernel builds and driver configuration, but the result—especially thunderbolt hotplugging and retina display support—makes it worthwhile for those committed to escaping macOS.

quotes.quote: I would just like to preface this by saying I do not condone cheating but I thought of this as a 'challenge' and not so much as 'cheating'.
quotes.summary: This early blog post from Jess Frazelle documents her exploration of Foursquare's API to enable remote check-ins for badge collection. She reverse-engineered the check-in process using PHP and cURL, discovering the rate limits and geographic validation rules Foursquare implemented. The post serves as both a technical tutorial on OAuth authentication and API manipulation, and an early example of her characteristic approach to treating security boundaries as puzzles to solve.
quotes.key_insight: An early glimpse of Frazelle's hacker mindset: treating platform restrictions as technical puzzles to understand and circumvent through API exploration.

quotes.quote: If you are not happy with the way things are currently going, you should stand up and try to change it.
quotes.summary: Inspired by a coffee shop sign asking 'What would 2PAC do?', Frazelle reflects on Tupac's song 'Changes' and draws parallels to her own life decision to take a new job in New York. She connects Tupac's message about change with Gandhi's philosophy about self-transformation, concluding that while external inspiration is valuable, ultimately you must trust yourself to make important life decisions.
quotes.key_insight: While external inspiration and role models can prompt reflection on major decisions, ultimately you must trust yourself as your own best advocate when choosing to embrace change.
